questions.q: Identify what a wave transfers as it travels through a medium.
options: Energy, without a net transfer of matter
questions.explanation: A wave transfers energy from place to place without a net transfer of matter — the particles of the medium oscillate about fixed points but don't travel with the wave (like a cork bobbing as waves pass). 'Energy not matter' is the single most important thing to say about what a wave is.

questions.q: Distinguish between a transverse and a longitudinal wave.
options: Transverse needs a medium; longitudinal does not
Transverse: particles oscillate perpendicular to travel; longitudinal: particles oscillate parallel to travel
Transverse: particles oscillate parallel to travel; longitudinal: perpendicular
They are the same except for speed
questions.explanation: In a transverse wave the particles oscillate perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of travel (e.g. a wave on a string, all EM waves). In a longitudinal wave they oscillate parallel to the travel, forming compressions and rarefactions (e.g. sound). The transverse/longitudinal split is about particle direction; the mechanical/EM split is a separate question about needing a medium.

questions.q: Classify a sound wave by both schemes.
questions.explanation: Sound is longitudinal (air molecules oscillate along the direction of travel, with compressions and rarefactions) and mechanical (it needs a medium, so there is no sound in a vacuum). Light, by contrast, is transverse and electromagnetic — needing no medium. This is why you see a distant explosion before you hear it.

questions.q: State the relationship between frequency and period.
options: Frequency equals wavelength divided by period
questions.explanation: Frequency and period are reciprocals: f = 1/T. A wave of frequency 2 Hz completes 2 cycles per second, so each cycle takes ½ second (the period). Frequency is measured in hertz (cycles per second) and period in seconds.

questions.q: A wave has a frequency of 50 Hz and a wavelength of 2 m. Calculate its speed.
questions.explanation: By the wave equation v = fλ = 50 × 2 = 100 m s⁻¹. The tempting 25 comes from dividing frequency by wavelength instead of multiplying. The wave equation links the three headline quantities and is used in almost every wave calculation.

questions.q: At a fixed wave speed, if the frequency is doubled, predict what happens to the wavelength.
questions.explanation: Since v = fλ and v is fixed (set by the medium, not the source), frequency and wavelength are inversely related — doubling the frequency halves the wavelength. This is why a higher-pitched sound has a shorter wavelength than a low one in the same air.

questions.q: Distinguish between the wave speed and the particle speed for a water wave.
options: Wave speed is how fast the disturbance travels; particle speed is how fast a particle oscillates about its rest position
Particle speed is how fast the pattern moves; wave speed is the oscillation
Wave speed is always slower than particle speed
questions.explanation: The wave speed (the v in v = fλ) is how fast the disturbance/pattern travels through the medium; the particle speed is how fast an individual particle oscillates back and forth about its rest position. They are unrelated — on the ocean the wave can race along while a water molecule just bobs slowly in place.

questions.q: Identify which type of wave graph you read the wavelength from.
options: Neither graph can give the wavelength
questions.explanation: A displacement-versus-position graph (a snapshot of all particles at one instant) gives the wavelength — the distance for one cycle. A displacement-versus-time graph gives the period (and hence frequency). Always check the horizontal axis first: time → period/frequency, position → wavelength.

questions.q: A displacement-time graph shows a period of 0.4 s, and a snapshot shows a wavelength of 0.8 m. Calculate the wave speed.
questions.explanation: From the time graph, f = 1/T = 1/0.4 = 2.5 Hz; from the snapshot, λ = 0.8 m. Then v = fλ = 2.5 × 0.8 = 2 m s⁻¹. You need both graphs: the time graph for frequency, the position graph for wavelength, then the wave equation to combine them.

questions.q: The amplitude of a wave is doubled. Determine the effect on the energy it carries.
questions.explanation: The energy a wave carries is proportional to the square of its amplitude, so doubling the amplitude quadruples the energy. This is why a slightly bigger ocean swell hits so much harder, and why a loud sound carries far more energy than a quiet one of the same pitch.
